The person behind the projects

VP by day. DIYer by every other spare moment.

I'm Marcie. I lead engineering teams for a living, and I renovate my house on weekends — sometimes both at the same time, poorly. This is the story of how I went from a hacksaw and a dream to a garage full of power tools and a house I genuinely love.

Read the projects
@marcieshouseofdiys
50+ projects & counting

The honest story

I didn't start with a vision. I started with a hacksaw.

When I did my first home project, I wasn't sure I was even going to enjoy this. So I kept my investment low: one hacksaw, one miter box, and a YouTube rabbit hole that lasted approximately three weekends.

The project worked. More importantly, I loved the process. The problem-solving. The satisfaction of standing back and seeing something that wasn't there before. That feeling, it turns out, is addictive.

I didn't buy a table saw until I was sure I needed one. I didn't buy a router until I understood what a router was for. Every tool I own was earned — the hard way, through experiments gone sideways and YouTube tutorials watched at 1.5x speed at midnight.

"A lot of YouTube. A lot of experiments gone awry. But overall just a lesson in rolling your sleeves up and giving it a go."

— Marcie, on the whole thing

The arsenal

From one hacksaw to a real workshop

Hacksaw & miter box
The humble beginning. Manual, slow, and honestly a great teacher.
Miter saw
The first power tool. Trim work was never the same again. [GAME CHANGER!]
Circular saw
For sheet goods, long cuts, and feeling like a professional.
Router
Edges, profiles, decorative details. A whole new level of craft.
Nail gun
Board and batten in half the time. Life-changing. Genuinely.

The table saw came somewhere in the middle — after I'd built enough things to know I'd be building more. Each tool arrived when the project demanded it, which meant I actually knew how to use it by the time I owned it. That's the DIY version of just-in-time learning, and it works.

How I approach it

The principles that actually guide these projects

01
Phases over perfection
Nothing in my house happened all at once. The best rooms built themselves over months — one right decision at a time. Incremental isn't a compromise. It's how you end up with a space that actually reflects you.
02
Real life, real schedule
I run an engineering team. I have a family. I don't have unlimited weekends. Every project here was designed around a life that's already full — which means they're designed around your life too.
03
Start before you're ready
I started with a hacksaw because I wasn't sure I'd stick with this. Turns out the best way to know if you'll enjoy something is to do it badly once first. The tools, the confidence, and the vision all come later.
04
YouTube is a valid education
I have learned more from YouTube than from any course, book, or contractor. There is a video for everything. Watch it twice, pause it constantly, and don't be embarrassed about either of those things.
05
This has so much potential
This is the thought that starts every project. Not "this is perfect" — not yet. Just the conviction that something better is possible, and that you're the person who can get it there. That's enough to begin.
06
The experiments gone awry
Some of my best decisions came right after my worst ones. The projects that didn't go as planned taught me more than the ones that did. The awry experiments are not failures — they're the curriculum.
